content.text: JavaScript classes (ES2015) provide a clean syntax for creating objects that share behaviour through a prototype chain. TypeScript extends them with access modifiers, parameter properties, abstract classes, and generics. Every Angular component, service, directive, and pipe is a TypeScript class.

attrs.alt: JavaScript/TypeScript class anatomy, inheritance, constructor shorthand, and access modifiers
attrs.title: JavaScript/TypeScript class anatomy, inheritance, constructor shorthand, and access modifiers

content.text: Declaring a constructor parameter with an access modifier automatically creates and assigns the property — no separate declaration needed.
Abstract classes cannot be instantiated directly — they define a contract and shared implementation that subclasses must complete.
Static properties and methods belong to the class constructor itself — not to instances. They are shared across all instances and are accessed via the class name.
Forgetting super() in a subclass constructor — JavaScript throws before this is usable
Using private when you need runtime privacy — TypeScript private is erased; use # for true privacy
Mutating readonly properties after construction — TypeScript catches this at compile time
Accessing static members via this — use the class name (ClassName.prop), not this.prop
Arrow class fields for every method — they create per-instance copies instead of shared prototype methods, increasing memory for large instance counts
Class fields (public and private) are declared directly on the class body, outside any method. Understanding when they initialise relative to the constructor prevents subtle bugs.
prefix) are part of the language spec — they differ from TypeScript's
keyword, which is erased at compile time.
JavaScript supports only single inheritance, but
let you compose behaviour from multiple sources by wrapping a base class in a function.
Mixins are useful in Angular for sharing behaviour (e.g. destroy lifecycle, loading state) across unrelated components or services without deep inheritance chains.
Angular services, components, and pipes are just classes. Testing them directly — without the full TestBed when possible — keeps tests fast and focused.
for tests that exercise Angular DI, lifecycle hooks, or the template — not for logic that is entirely in the class body.
Forgetting super() in a subclass constructor
ReferenceError: must call super before accessing this
Always call super(...) as the first line
Arrow properties are own-properties — they shadow prototype methods and cannot be overridden
Use regular methods for overridable behaviour
Tests share the same static values and bleed state into each other
Reset static fields in afterEach, or prefer instance state
Relying on TypeScript private at runtime
Other code (or the DI system via reflection) can still access it via any cast
Use # private fields when true encapsulation is required
Couples unrelated behaviour; makes subclasses hard to test
Prefer composition (services, mixins) over deep hierarchies
JavaScript classes provide clean syntax for object-oriented patterns over the prototype system. TypeScript adds access modifiers (
, abstract classes, and generics. Angular components, services, guards, and pipes are all TypeScript classes decorated with Angular-specific metadata. Understand that
for true runtime privacy when it matters.
